## SECTION 7 — This Day in History (World)
On January 25, 1971, Idi Amin, a massive, highly aggressive, and deeply ruthless military commander, successfully launched a massive military coup d'état in Uganda, overthrowing the government of President Milton Obote. Amin's subsequent, massive eight-year dictatorship was characterized by extreme, massive brutality, widespread human rights abuses, massive economic mismanagement, and the systematic, massive ethnic cleansing and expulsion of the entire Asian population from Uganda, resulting in the massive, horrific deaths of up to 500,000 people.

On January 25, 1924, the first-ever massive Winter Olympic Games were officially opened in the picturesque alpine resort of Chamonix, France. Originally organized as a massive "International Winter Sports Week" in association with the 1924 Summer Olympics in Paris, the event was a massive, undisputed success. It was retroactively designated by the International Olympic Committee as the first official Winter Olympics, fundamentally establishing a massive, permanent global platform for competitive winter sports.

## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (India)
On January 25, 1950, just one day before the massive, historic adoption of the Constitution, the Election Commission of India was formally established. This massive, permanent, and highly independent constitutional body was granted massive, absolute authority to superintend, direct, and control the entire, massive process of conducting elections to the national Parliament, state legislatures, and the offices of the President and Vice-President, ensuring the massive, foundational integrity of the world's largest democracy.

On January 25, 1971, the massive, mountainous northern territory of Himachal Pradesh was officially granted full statehood, becoming the 18th massive state of the Indian Union. Previously administered directly by the central government as a massive Union Territory, the elevation to statehood granted the region massive, highly demanded political autonomy. This massive political restructuring allowed the state to directly govern its massive, unique Himalayan ecology and distinct regional culture through its own elected legislative assembly.
